title: Casual or polite?
pedagogy: Register: casual หวัดดี (ห-nam high+dead→low, mid+live→mid) vs polite สวัสดี+particle vs formal ลาก่อน (low+live→mid, mid+mai ek→low); คุณ (low+live→mid) as the polite you. Minimal pair เสือ/เสื้อ/เสื่อ covers high-class unmarked/mai tho/mai ek. Reviews unit 1 politeness vocabulary.

exercises.title: Reading the room
body: Thai has gears: casual with friends, particle-polite with strangers, formal for weight. Choosing the right gear IS speaking Thai well.
Rough guide: friends and peers → casual, no particle needed. Anyone serving you, older, or unknown → polite particle. Officials, ceremonies, real farewells → formal.
When unsure, go one gear more polite. Nobody is offended by extra courtesy.

exercises.context: You're messaging a close Thai friend to say hi.
exercises.question: Which greeting fits?

choices.thai: สวัสดีครับ

exercises.explanation: Friends get หวัดดี — สวัสดีครับ isn't wrong, it just reads stiff in a chat, like opening a text with “Good day, sir.” ลาก่อน is a goodbye, and a heavy one.

exercises.context: First day at a new job in Bangkok. You're a woman, greeting your boss.
exercises.question: What do you say?
exercises.explanation: Boss + first impression = full polite form: สวัสดีค่ะ. หวัดดี is for peers — and sticking a ค่ะ on it doesn't dress it up enough for this room.

exercises.context: You're a man leaving a shop after a chat with the owner — you'll likely be back next week.
exercises.question: How do you say goodbye?

choices.thai: สวัสดีครับ

exercises.explanation: สวัสดี closes an everyday encounter as smoothly as it opens one. ลาก่อน announces a long parting — save it for airports and endings, not the fruit stand.
